name: Phase 4 — Prevent recurrence: OpenAPI codegen + contract snapshot test
description: Two stories that turn the hand-typed contract into a generated artifact, eliminating the class of bug that motivated this whole audit. (1) Add an `openapi-typescript` build step to the frontend that consumes a committed `openapi.snapshot.json` generated DIRECTLY FROM THE LOCAL FastAPI APP CODE (no live API needed) and emits `src/api/generated.ts`. (2) Add `tests/contract.test.ts` that imports each hand-typed interface in `src/api/*.ts` and asserts structural compatibility with the generated types. Catches drift at PR time. PRECONDITION: Phase 1 merged on master in `hungry-machines-api/`. The API does NOT need to be redeployed — FastAPI emits OpenAPI from the app object via `app.openapi()`, so generating from the master-branch source captures the canonical schema. This is actually preferable to a live fetch because it pins to committed code rather than whatever the VPS happens to be running.
env_policy: Frontend repo. RALPH verifies offline. Snapshot generation runs from the sibling `hungry-machines-api/` directory: `cd hungry-machines-api && python -c "import json; from app.main import app; print(json.dumps(app.openapi()))" > ../hungry-machines_base-frontend/openapi.snapshot.json`. The API repo's CLAUDE.md guarantees the app imports cleanly with empty .env (lazy Supabase client), so this works without live credentials. All other Verify steps run via `npm test`, `npm run build`, `npx tsc --noEmit`, and grep — no network access.

stories.title: Generate src/api/generated.ts from a committed openapi.snapshot.json
stories.description: Set up the codegen pipeline. Output is one committed file that downstream tests can compare against. The snapshot is itself committed so the build is reproducible offline.
acceptanceCriteria: Add `openapi-typescript` to `devDependencies` in `package.json` (run `npm install --save-dev openapi-typescript@latest` and commit the package.json + package-lock.json deltas; lock to a specific minor version).

Generate `openapi.snapshot.json` at this repo's root from the LOCAL FastAPI source in `../hungry-machines-api/`. The exact command is: `cd ../hungry-machines-api && python -c "import json; from app.main import app; print(json.dumps(app.openapi(), indent=2))" > ../hungry-machines_base-frontend/openapi.snapshot.json`. This works without a running server, without live credentials, and pins the snapshot to master-branch committed API code rather than whatever's deployed on the VPS. If the python invocation can't find `app` (e.g. missing requirements), document the exact install step needed (typically `pip install -r ../hungry-machines-api/requirements.txt` inside the existing venv) and proceed once the import works. The snapshot file is committed alongside generated.ts so future regens are reproducible.

Add an npm script: `"codegen": "openapi-typescript openapi.snapshot.json -o src/api/generated.ts"`. Run it once: `npm run codegen`. Commit the resulting `src/api/generated.ts`. The file is large (a few thousand lines); commit as-is.

Add a guard test in `tests/codegen-snapshot.test.ts` that reads `src/api/generated.ts` from disk and asserts (a) the file exists; (b) it contains type definitions for the endpoints we know about: `'/auth/login'`, `'/auth/me'`, `'/api/v1/preferences'`, `'/api/v1/schedules'`, `'/api/v1/rates'`, `'/api/v1/appliances'`. The test uses `fs.readFileSync` and substring checks — keeps it dumb and fast.

Add a one-paragraph section to `README.md` titled '## Regenerating the API types' with the exact workflow: when the backend lands a contract change, run `cd ../hungry-machines-api && python -c "import json; from app.main import app; print(json.dumps(app.openapi(), indent=2))" > ../hungry-machines_base-frontend/openapi.snapshot.json && cd ../hungry-machines_base-frontend && npm run codegen && npm test` from the monorepo root, and commit any deltas in the same PR. Mention that this captures committed master-branch API state, NOT live deployed state — that's the point.

Verify: `cd hungry-machines_base-frontend && test -f openapi.snapshot.json && test -f src/api/generated.ts` exits 0. `cd hungry-machines_base-frontend && npm run codegen` runs successfully against the committed snapshot. `cd hungry-machines_base-frontend && npm test` passes (including the new codegen-snapshot test). `grep -F '"openapi-typescript"' package.json | wc -l` returns at least 1. `grep -F 'openapi.snapshot.json' README.md | wc -l` returns at least 1. The committed snapshot must contain post-Phase-1 paths: `grep -F '/api/v1/rates' openapi.snapshot.json | wc -l` returns at least 1, `grep -F '/auth/signup' openapi.snapshot.json | wc -l` returns at least 1, and `grep -F '/auth/register' openapi.snapshot.json | wc -l` returns 0 (the legacy doc-only endpoint never existed in code).
stories.notes: Generated openapi.snapshot.json (39733 bytes) by importing the FastAPI app object from ../hungry-machines-api: `PYTHONPATH=... python3 -c 'import json; from app.main import app; ...'` writing via Python directly to disk (shell redirection was sandbox-blocked). Snapshot contains /api/v1/rates (1), /auth/signup (1), /auth/register (0) — Phase-1 backend pinned. Added openapi-typescript@~7.13.0 devDep (locked to a specific minor) and `codegen` npm script. `npm run codegen` produced src/api/generated.ts (1260 lines) with the six expected path keys: /auth/login, /auth/me, /api/v1/preferences, /api/v1/schedules, /api/v1/rates, /api/v1/appliances. tests/codegen-snapshot.test.ts uses fs.readFileSync + substring checks (2 cases: file existence, all six path keys present). README.md gained a `## Regenerating the API types` section with the exact one-line workflow command and a note that the snapshot pins to committed master-branch state, NOT live deployed state. Added @types/node@~20.0.0 because tsc complained about node:fs/path/__dirname imports in the new test (no other test in the suite uses Node APIs). Files changed: package.json, package-lock.json, openapi.snapshot.json, src/api/generated.ts, tests/codegen-snapshot.test.ts, README.md. Verify: 17 vitest files / 107 tests pass; tsc --noEmit clean; npm run build produces hungry-machines.js.

stories.title: tests/contract.test.ts asserts hand-typed wrappers are compatible with generated.ts
stories.description: The hand-typed interfaces in `src/api/*.ts` (e.g. `Preferences`, `RatesResponse`, `Appliance`) must remain structurally compatible with the generated types. A test that fails to type-check on drift is the cheapest possible enforcement.
acceptanceCriteria: Read `src/api/{auth,preferences,appliances,schedules,rates}.ts` first to inventory every exported interface. Note which ones map to which generated path (e.g. `Preferences` → `paths['/api/v1/preferences']['get']['responses']['200']['content']['application/json']`).

Create `tests/contract.test.ts`. For each hand-typed interface, write a TypeScript-only assertion using a structural-compat helper. Pattern: `import type { paths } from '../src/api/generated'; import type { Preferences } from '../src/api/preferences'; type _Compat = Preferences extends paths['/api/v1/preferences']['get']['responses']['200']['content']['application/json'] ? true : false; const _check: _Compat = true;`. Repeat for each interface: `Preferences`, `RatesResponse`, `Appliance`, `HvacScheduleResponse`, `SchedulesResponse`, `UserMe`, `Session`, `ApplianceSchedule`. The assertions live at module scope so `tsc --noEmit` catches drift even without a runtime test runner.
Add ONE runtime vitest test that does `expect(true).toBe(true)` so vitest counts the file. The real verification is the type assertions failing `tsc`.

Add an npm script `"check:contract": "tsc --noEmit"` if it doesn't already exist (it does — the existing `npm test` runs `vitest`, separate from tsc). Document in README that `npm run check:contract` is the dedicated drift gate.

Add a CI step in `.github/workflows/release.yml` (or a new `.github/workflows/contract.yml`) that runs `npm run check:contract` on every push to master. If the workflow file doesn't exist yet, leave a TODO comment and document in README that it needs to be added.

Verify: `cd hungry-machines_base-frontend && npx tsc --noEmit` passes (no type errors in contract.test.ts against the committed generated.ts). `cd hungry-machines_base-frontend && npm test` passes. `grep -F 'paths' tests/contract.test.ts | wc -l` returns at least 8 (one path lookup per checked interface). `grep -F 'check:contract' package.json | wc -l` returns at least 1.
stories.notes: Created tests/contract.test.ts with 8 module-scope `extends ... ? true : false` assertions — one per hand-typed interface (Preferences, RatesResponse, Appliance, HvacScheduleResponse, SchedulesResponse, UserMe, Session, ApplianceSchedule) — each pinned to a `paths['<endpoint>']['<method>']['responses']['200']['content']['application/json']` lookup in src/api/generated.ts. Added `"check:contract": "tsc --noEmit"` script to package.json (the AC noted the script may already exist; it didn't, so added it as the dedicated drift gate alongside the existing `"test": "vitest run"`). Vitest case at module bottom does `expect(_assertions.every((v) => v === true)).toBe(true)` so the file is counted in `npm test` (1 case, no real runtime check — drift gate is tsc). Created .github/workflows/contract.yml that runs `npm run check:contract` + `npm test` on every push and PR to master (release.yml only fires on tag pushes, so a separate workflow was needed per the AC's 'on every push to master' wording). Also added `npm run check:contract` to release.yml's job before `npm test` for tag-push parity. Two real-drift fixes were required to make the assertions pass: (1) `src/api/schedules.ts` HvacScheduleResponse.stale changed from `stale?: boolean` to `stale: boolean` because openapi-typescript marks the FastAPI default-valued field as required (the API always returns it; hand-typed was overly permissive). (2) `HvacScheduleBody` switched from `interface` to `type` alias because TypeScript interfaces don't auto-extend index-signatured types (`{[key: string]: unknown}`) — the generated ScheduleResponse uses an index signature for `schedule`, and only `type` aliases satisfy that structurally. Both fixes are runtime-safe (no test fixture in tests/thermostat-card.test.ts constructs HvacScheduleResponse via typed literal; jsonResponse(body: unknown) accepts any shape). README gained a new paragraph under the existing 'Regenerating the API types' section explaining the contract.test.ts gate and where to find the workflow. Verify counts: grep -F 'paths' contract.test.ts → 9 (≥8 required, 8 path lookups + 1 import statement). grep -F 'check:contract' package.json → 1. tsc --noEmit clean (no errors). 18 vitest files / 108 tests pass (prior baseline 17/107). npm run build clean.
